quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Power rule for all exponents"?

options.text: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Power rule for all exponents" is defined as: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Negative exponent derivatives"?

options.text: d/dx[x⁻ⁿ] = -nx⁻ⁿ⁻¹; equivalently d/dx[1/xⁿ] = -n/x^(n+1)
Numerical approximation: start at (x₀,y₀), step by yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + f(xₙ,yₙ)·Δx with step size Δx
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Negative exponent derivatives" is defined as: d/dx[x⁻ⁿ] = -nx⁻ⁿ⁻¹; equivalently d/dx[1/xⁿ] = -n/x^(n+1). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Fractional exponent derivatives"?

options.text: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
For definite integrals: convert limits from x to u (u_lower = g(a), u_upper = g(b)); no need to substitute back
A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
d/dx[x^(p/q)] = (p/q)x^(p/q - 1); applies to roots: d/dx[∛x] = d/dx[x^(1/3)] = (1/3)x^(-2/3)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Fractional exponent derivatives" is defined as: d/dx[x^(p/q)] = (p/q)x^(p/q - 1); applies to roots: d/dx[∛x] = d/dx[x^(1/3)] = (1/3)x^(-2/3). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Exponential function"?

options.text: Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel
For triply composed functions: d/dx[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x)
d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ; the only function that is its own derivative (up to a constant multiple)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Exponential function" is defined as: d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ; the only function that is its own derivative (up to a constant multiple). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Natural logarithm"?

options.text: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x for x > 0; extends to d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
EVT guarantees extrema exist but does not tell us where; we use critical point analysis and endpoint evaluation to locate them
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives
Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Natural logarithm" is defined as: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x for x > 0; extends to d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Power Rule and Basic Derivatives is accurate?

options.text: The solution is P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀.
Power Rule and Basic Derivatives extends differentiation to polynomial and simple algebraic functions.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.
The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power Rule and Basic Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.
The power rule d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ works for all real exponents, including negative integers (d/dx[x⁻²] = -2x⁻³) and rational exponents (d/dx[√x] = d/dx[x^(1/2)] = (1/2)x^(-1/2)).
Work against gravity: W = ∫mgy'(x)dx for lifting objects along curves.
These combined techniques are essential for related rates problems and advanced curve analysis.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power Rule and Basic Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.
Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.
Combined with constant multiple and sum rules, any polynomial can be differentiated term by term.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power Rule and Basic Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Additional basic derivatives: d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ, d/dx[ln x] = 1/x.
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).
These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power Rule and Basic Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Trigonometric substitutions handle expressions with √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²).
Power Rule and Basic Derivatives extends differentiation to polynomial and simple algebraic functions.
Visualizing MVT helps interpret derivative graphs and connect graphical and analytical representations.
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power Rule and Basic Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Radioactive decay: if N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), then N'(t) = -λN₀e^(-λt), showing the decay rate is proportional to the current amount—a hallmark of exponential decay
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model
Quality control: using normal distribution integrals to determine the probability that a manufactured part falls within specifications (Six Sigma methodology)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations
Dam design: engineers compute total hydrostatic force on dam faces to determine required structural strength and materials
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Power Rule and Basic Derivatives applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Fluid dynamics: the velocity profile in a pipe follows a power law, and its derivative gives the shear rate at any radial position
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives?

options.text: Structural engineering: the second derivative of a beam's deflection function indicates whether a critical point is a maximum deflection (sag) or minimum
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model
Radioactive decay: if N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), then N'(t) = -λN₀e^(-λt), showing the decay rate is proportional to the current amount—a hallmark of exponential decay
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Power Rule and Basic Derivatives in practice?

options.text: Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power Rule and Basic Derivatives.
